facts: The Latin American independence movements were inspired by the American and French Revolutions because those revolutions demonstrated that colonies could successfully overthrow European rulers.
Simón Bolívar led independence movements in northern South America, while José de San Martín led them in the southern part, and they coordinated efforts to free the continent from Spanish rule.
Mexico gained independence from Spain in 1821, not through a single event but after a decade-long war that began with Miguel Hidalgo's call in 1810.
Before independence, Latin American colonies were ruled by Spain, meaning they had to obey Spanish laws and send wealth to Spain, which created resentment among colonists.

The independence movements in Latin America occurred in the early 1800s, about 30 years after the American Revolution and shortly after the French Revolution, showing a wave of revolutionary ideas across the Atlantic.
misconceptions: Students believe that all Latin American countries became independent from Spain in the same year, such as 1821, but in reality independence was achieved at different times across the region.
Students think that Simón Bolívar alone liberated all of South America, ignoring the role of José de San Martín in the southern campaigns.
